Nurses must address confidentiality breaches using professional and private communication. Corrective feedback should be given respectfully and in a confidential setting to maintain professionalism and patient privacy.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Confidentiality and Professional Communication

answerChoiceList.choice: Praise the LPN for talking to the client about the diagnostic test.
Tell the LPN the nurse needs to talk to her in the office area.
Go to the waiting room and tell the LPN not to discuss this there.
Inform the HCP that the LPN was talking to the client in the waiting room.

questionList.questionText: The charge nurse in a large outpatient clinic notices the staff members are arguing and irritable with one another and the atmosphere has been very tense for the past week. Which action should the charge nurse take?
questionList.explanationText:
Effective leadership and conflict management are essential roles of a charge nurse. Workplace conflict can negatively affect team collaboration, patient safety, and quality of care. The nurse leader should use open communication, problem-solving strategies, and team involvement to address issues. A proactive approach such as holding a meeting allows staff to express concerns, identify underlying causes, and develop solutions collaboratively. Avoiding the issue or using authoritarian approaches can worsen morale and increase tension. Constructive communication promotes a positive work environment, mutual respect, and improved teamwork.

(Correct Option 3). Scheduling a meeting with staff is the most appropriate action because it encourages open dialogue and collaborative conflict resolution. It allows the charge nurse to address concerns directly, promote understanding, and develop solutions as a team.

(Option 1). Waiting for the situation to resolve on its own is inappropriate because unresolved conflict can escalate and negatively impact staff morale and patient care. Active intervention is required.

(Option 2). Writing a memo is ineffective because it does not allow for two-way communication or problem-solving. It may be perceived as impersonal and fail to address the root cause.

(Option 4). Threatening termination is an authoritarian and punitive approach that can increase fear and resentment, further worsening the work environment rather than resolving the conflict.

Educational Objective
Nurse leaders should use collaborative communication and conflict resolution strategies to address workplace issues. Open discussion promotes teamwork, improves morale, and ensures a safe patient care environment.
questionList.subject: Leadership and Management
questionList.title: Conflict Resolution

answerChoiceList.choice: Wait for another week to see whether the situation resolves itself.
Write a memo telling all staff members to stop arguing.
Schedule a meeting with the staff to discuss the situation.
Tell the staff to stop arguing or they will be terminated.

questionList.questionText: The employee health nurse is obtaining a urine specimen for a pre-employment drug screen. Which action should the nurse implement first?
questionList.explanationText:
In drug screening procedures, maintaining the chain of custody is critical to ensure the legal integrity and validity of the specimen. The chain of custody refers to the documented process that tracks the collection, handling, storage, and transfer of the specimen to prevent tampering, contamination, or misidentification. This process is especially important in pre-employment drug testing, where results may have legal and occupational consequences. Any break in this chain can render the specimen invalid. Nurses must follow strict protocols, including proper labeling, sealing, and documentation, before and during specimen collection to maintain accuracy and credibility.

(Correct Option 2). Maintaining the chain of custody is the priority and must be implemented first to ensure the specimen is legally defensible and has not been altered. Proper documentation and secure handling begin at the time of collection and continue throughout the process.

(Option 1). Obtaining informed consent is important, but in routine pre-employment drug screening, consent is typically implied or already obtained as part of employment requirements. It is not the immediate priority over maintaining specimen integrity.

(Option 3). Allowing the client to go to any bathroom is inappropriate because it may compromise the security and integrity of the specimen. Controlled environments are required for accurate testing.

(Option 4). Taking and recording tympanic temperature may be done to detect specimen tampering, but it is not the first priority compared to establishing and maintaining the chain of custody.

Educational Objective
In drug testing, maintaining the chain of custody is the highest priority to ensure specimen integrity and legal validity. Proper handling and documentation must begin immediately at the time of collection.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.topic: Specimen Collection and Chain of Custody
questionList.title: Specimen Collection and Chain of Custody

answerChoiceList.choice: Obtain informed consent for the procedure.
Maintain the chain of custody for the specimen.
Allow the client to go to any bathroom in the clinic.
Take and record the client’s tympanic temperature.

questionList.questionText: The client comes to the clinic reporting pain and burning on urination. Which action should the nurse implement first?
questionList.explanationText:
Clients presenting with dysuria (painful urination) often have a suspected urinary tract infection (UTI). The priority nursing action is to obtain accurate diagnostic data before initiating further assessment or interventions. A midstream urine specimen (clean-catch) is essential to identify bacteria, white blood cells, and possible infection without contamination. Early specimen collection ensures reliable laboratory results and guides appropriate treatment. Delaying specimen collection may lead to inaccurate results due to contamination or prior voiding. Nursing care follows the principle of assessment before intervention, but obtaining a clean specimen is critical for confirming the diagnosis.

(Correct Option 3). Requesting a midstream urine specimen is the priority because it provides accurate diagnostic information needed to confirm a UTI. This step should be done first before additional questioning or interventions to avoid compromising specimen integrity.

(Option 1). Assessing vital signs is important but not the immediate priority in this stable client. There are no signs of systemic infection such as fever or instability requiring urgent vital assessment first.

(Option 2). Asking about blood in the urine is part of a thorough history but does not take priority over obtaining a diagnostic specimen needed for confirmation.

(Option 4). Asking about hygiene practices is relevant for patient education but is not the first step. It should follow after diagnosis and assessment.

Educational Objective
When a UTI is suspected, obtaining a clean-catch midstream urine specimen is the priority to ensure accurate diagnosis and guide treatment. Early specimen collection prevents contamination and improves reliability of results.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.topic: Urinary Tract Infection and Specimen Collection
questionList.title: Urinary Tract Infection and Specimen Collection

answerChoiceList.choice: Assess and document the client’s vital signs.
Determine whether the client has seen any blood in the urine.
Request the client give a midstream urine specimen.
Ask the client whether she wipes front to back after a bowel movement.

questionList.questionText: The nurse is working at the emergency health clinic in a disaster shelter. Which intervention is priority when initially assessing the client?
questionList.explanationText:
In a disaster or emergency shelter setting, nursing care follows principles of triage, prioritization, and immediate safety needs. Clients may have limited access to essential medications, which can lead to rapid deterioration of chronic conditions such as diabetes, hypertension, asthma, or cardiac disorders. The nurse must quickly identify life-sustaining needs and prevent complications caused by missed medications. According to disaster nursing principles, ensuring access to critical medications and treatments takes precedence over routine assessments. Early identification allows prompt intervention, reduces morbidity, and ensures continuity of care in unstable environments.

(Correct Option 2). Determining whether the client has routine medications is the priority because missing essential medications can result in immediate health risks and exacerbation of chronic illnesses. This assessment allows the nurse to quickly arrange for medication replacement or continuation of therapy.

(Option 1). Asking how long the client will stay in the shelter is not a priority during the initial assessment. It does not address immediate health or safety needs.

(Option 3). Documenting the client’s health history is important but can be done after addressing urgent needs. It is not the first priority in disaster triage.

(Option 4). Assessing vital signs, height, and weight is part of a general assessment, but it is not as critical as identifying lack of essential medications, which can lead to acute complications.

Educational Objective
In disaster settings, nurses must prioritize life-sustaining needs, including access to essential medications. Early identification of medication needs prevents complications and ensures continuity of care.
questionList.subject: Emergency Nursing
questionList.title: Triage and Prioritization

answerChoiceList.choice: Find out how long the client will be in the shelter.
Determine whether the client has his or her routine medications.
Document the client’s health history in writing.
Assess the client’s vital signs, height, and weight.

questionList.questionText: The HCP orders an intravenous pyelogram for the 27-year-old male client diagnosed with R/O renal calculi. The client is diagnosed with schizophrenia and is delusional. Which action should the clinic nurse implement?
questionList.explanationText:
Obtaining informed consent is a critical legal and ethical responsibility in healthcare. For consent to be valid, the client must have decision-making capacity, meaning the ability to understand the procedure, its risks, benefits, and alternatives. Clients with altered mental status, such as those experiencing delusions due to schizophrenia, may lack the capacity to provide informed consent. In such cases, a legally authorized representative (LAR), such as a significant other or guardian, must provide consent. Nurses are responsible for ensuring that consent is obtained appropriately before procedures to protect patient rights and legal standards.

(Correct Option 3). Obtaining informed consent from the client’s significant other is appropriate because the client is delusional and may lack the capacity to provide valid consent. A legally authorized representative must give consent to ensure ethical and legal compliance.

(Option 1). Asking about a yeast allergy is incorrect because intravenous pyelogram contrast is related to iodine or shellfish sensitivity, not yeast. This question is irrelevant to the procedure.

(Option 2). Requesting the client to sign the permit is inappropriate because the client is not competent to provide informed consent due to delusional thinking.

(Option 4). Discussing the day surgery procedure is helpful for education, but it does not address the priority issue of obtaining valid informed consent before the procedure.

Educational Objective
Clients must have decision-making capacity to provide informed consent. If impaired, a legally authorized representative must consent to ensure ethical and legal care.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Informed Consent and Mental Capacity

answerChoiceList.choice: Ask the client whether he is allergic to yeast.
Request the client to sign a permit for the procedure.
Obtain informed consent from the client’s significant other.
Discuss the local hospital’s day surgery procedure with the client.

questionList.questionText: The home health (HH) aide tells the home health nurse one of the older male clients is taking an herbal supplement, saw palmetto, every day. Which statement is the nurse’s best response?
questionList.explanationText:
Saw palmetto is a commonly used herbal supplement that has been traditionally used to manage symptoms of benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), such as urinary frequency, hesitancy, and nocturia. In nursing practice, it is important to recognize that many clients use complementary and alternative therapies. Nurses should demonstrate cultural competence, respect for client autonomy, and evidence-based knowledge when addressing such therapies. While some herbal supplements may interact with medications, not all are inherently harmful. The nurse’s role includes assessing, educating, and monitoring, rather than immediately discouraging use without evidence of harm.

(Correct Option 2). Saw palmetto is commonly used to treat symptoms of benign prostatic hypertrophy, and acknowledging this demonstrates knowledge and acceptance of complementary therapies. The nurse supports the client’s choice while remaining aware of its purpose and monitoring for any interactions.

(Option 1). Stating that herbal supplements are dangerous is incorrect and overly generalized. This response lacks evidence-based practice and may undermine client trust.

(Option 3). Notifying the healthcare provider immediately is unnecessary unless there are adverse effects or potential drug interactions. This action is not the priority.

(Option 4). While clients do have the right to take supplements, this response does not demonstrate clinical knowledge or provide appropriate guidance about the supplement’s use.

Educational Objective
Nurses should recognize common herbal supplements and their uses, support client autonomy, and provide informed guidance while monitoring for potential interactions or adverse effects.
questionList.subject: Pharmacology
questionList.title: Herbal Medications and BPH

answerChoiceList.choice: “Herbal supplements are dangerous and I will talk to the client.”
“Saw palmetto is used to treat benign prostatic hypertrophy. Let him take it.”
“I will notify the client’s healthcare provider as soon as possible.”
“Many clients use herbal supplements. He has a right to take it.”

questionList.questionText: The home health (HH) aide caring for the client who is postoperative kidney transplant asks the home health nurse, “Why is the physical therapist coming to visit the client?” Which statement is the home health nurse’s best response?
questionList.explanationText:
After a kidney transplant, clients often experience decreased mobility, muscle weakness, and reduced endurance due to surgery, hospitalization, and recovery processes. Physical therapy plays a vital role in promoting functional mobility, strength, and overall physical recovery. Physical therapists focus on improving muscle strength, balance, coordination, and endurance, which helps prevent complications such as deconditioning, falls, and delayed recovery. Interdisciplinary care ensures that each healthcare professional addresses specific needs within their scope of practice, enhancing holistic patient outcomes.

(Correct Option 4). The physical therapist works with the client on strengthening and endurance, which is essential for postoperative recovery. This helps improve mobility, prevent complications, and support the client’s return to normal function.

(Option 1). Evaluating swallowing difficulty is the role of a speech-language pathologist, not a physical therapist.

(Option 2). Assisting with fine motor coordination is typically the role of an occupational therapist, who focuses on activities of daily living.

(Option 3). Addressing caregiver concerns and making referrals is generally the responsibility of a social worker or case manager, not a physical therapist.

Educational Objective
Physical therapists focus on strength, mobility, and endurance to promote recovery and prevent complications. Understanding interdisciplinary roles ensures appropriate patient care and collaboration.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Interdisciplinary Team Roles

answerChoiceList.choice: “The physical therapist will evaluate the client’s swallowing difficulty.”
“The physical therapist will assist the client with fine motor coordination.”
“The physical therapist will assist with caregiver concerns and making referrals.”
“The physical therapist will work with the client on strengthening and endurance.”

questionList.questionText: The home health (HH) nurse is admitting a female client diagnosed with end-stage renal disease who refuses to be placed on hemodialysis. The client is ready to die, but verbalizes having so many regrets in her life. Which intervention would be most appropriate for the nurse?
questionList.explanationText:
Clients with end-stage renal disease (ESRD) who decline life-sustaining treatment such as dialysis are often experiencing end-of-life concerns, including spiritual distress, unresolved issues, and feelings of regret. According to holistic nursing care, addressing the psychosocial and spiritual needs of the client is essential. Spiritual care providers, such as chaplains, are specially trained to help clients explore meaning, cope with guilt, and find peace at the end of life. The nurse should recognize cues of spiritual distress and facilitate appropriate support while respecting the client’s beliefs and preferences. This promotes dignity, emotional comfort, and quality end-of-life care.

(Correct Option 1). Contacting the agency chaplain is the most appropriate intervention because the client is expressing spiritual distress and unresolved regrets. A chaplain is trained to provide spiritual counseling, emotional support, and guidance, helping the client cope with end-of-life concerns.

(Option 2). Calling the client’s pastor without permission violates confidentiality and autonomy. The nurse must first obtain consent before contacting outside individuals.

(Option 3). Asking to pray with the client may be supportive, but it assumes the nurse’s beliefs align with the client’s. This may not be appropriate unless the client initiates or expresses interest in prayer.

(Option 4). Determining whether the client has an advance directive is important for planning care, but it does not address the client’s immediate spiritual and emotional distress.

Educational Objective
Nurses should recognize spiritual distress in end-of-life clients and facilitate appropriate referrals, such as to a chaplain, to provide holistic and compassionate care.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: End-of-Life Care and Spiritual Support

answerChoiceList.choice: Contact the agency chaplain to come talk to the client.
Call her church pastor and discuss the client’s concerns.
Ask the client whether or not she would like to pray with the nurse.
Determine whether or not the client has an advance directive.

questionList.questionText: The nurse is preparing to perform a dressing change on a female client who has end-stage renal disease. The nurse notes the client’s husband is silently holding the client’s hand and praying. Which action should the nurse implement first?
questionList.explanationText:
In end-of-life or serious illness situations such as end-stage renal disease (ESRD), clients and families often engage in spiritual or religious practices as a coping mechanism. Nursing care must be patient-centered, culturally sensitive, and respectful of spiritual needs. Interrupting prayer or intimate family moments can increase emotional distress and violate the principles of holistic and compassionate care. According to nursing ethics, the nurse should prioritize respect for dignity, privacy, and emotional support over non-urgent procedures. Allowing uninterrupted spiritual expression helps promote peace, coping, and emotional well-being for both the client and family.

(Correct Option 3). Quietly leaving the room and returning later is the most appropriate action because it respects the client’s and family’s spiritual practice, privacy, and emotional needs. The dressing change is not an emergency and can be delayed to support holistic care and dignity.

(Option 1). Continuing preparation in the room may interrupt the prayer and shows lack of respect for the client’s spiritual needs. Non-urgent procedures should not take priority over emotional support.

(Option 2). Calling the chaplain without being asked may violate client autonomy and is unnecessary since the family is already engaged in prayer.

(Option 4). Asking to join the prayer may be inappropriate because it imposes the nurse’s presence during a private and intimate moment, unless explicitly invited by the client or family.

Educational Objective
Nurses must respect spiritual practices and emotional needs by avoiding interruption of prayer or family moments. Non-urgent care should be delayed to provide holistic, patient-centered care.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Spiritual Care and Patient-Centered Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Continue to prepare for the dressing change in the room.
Call the chaplain to help the client and spouse pray.
Quietly leave the room and come back later for the dressing change.
Ask the husband whether or not he would like the nurse to join in the prayer.

questionList.questionText: The unit manager on the renal unit is evaluating the staff nurse. Which data should be included in the nurse’s yearly evaluation?
questionList.explanationText:
Performance evaluation in nursing is a systematic process that assesses a nurse’s professional competencies, clinical skills, documentation accuracy, communication, and adherence to standards of care. It focuses on objective, measurable, and job-related behaviors rather than isolated or insignificant incidents. Effective evaluations emphasize quality of care, patient safety, accountability, and compliance with institutional policies. Documentation is a critical component because it reflects clinical judgment, legal accountability, and continuity of care. Therefore, evaluations should prioritize consistent performance indicators that directly impact patient outcomes and professional standards.

(Correct Option 4). The appropriateness of the nurse’s written documentation in the charts is a key indicator of professional competence. Accurate, timely, and complete documentation reflects the nurse’s clinical judgment and adherence to legal and professional standards. It directly impacts patient safety, continuity of care, and legal protection, making it essential for inclusion in a yearly evaluation.

(Option 1). Clocking in late twice in a year is a minor and infrequent occurrence. Such isolated incidents do not represent overall job performance and are not significant enough for inclusion in a comprehensive yearly evaluation.

(Option 2). A single complaint about not answering a call light during a code lacks context and may not reflect the nurse’s usual performance. Evaluations should not rely on isolated or subjective complaints without a pattern of behavior.

(Option 3). The number of times the nurse switched shifts is not directly related to job performance or patient care quality. As long as policies are followed, this behavior does not indicate competency or professional effectiveness.

Educational Objective
Yearly nursing evaluations should focus on objective, job-related performance measures such as documentation quality, clinical competence, and adherence to standards. Avoid including isolated incidents or non-performance-related behaviors that do not reflect overall professional practice.
questionList.subject: Leadership and Management
questionList.title: Performance Evaluation

answerChoiceList.choice: The fact that the nurse clocked in late to work twice in the last year.
The complaint stating the nurse did not answer a call light during a code.
The number of times the nurse switched shifts with another nurse.
The appropriateness of the nurse’s written documentation in the charts.

questionList.questionText: The hospice nurse is providing follow-up care with the family member of a client who died with chronic renal disease. Which intervention is priority?
questionList.explanationText:
Hospice care focuses on providing holistic, end-of-life support not only to the client but also to the family, including during the bereavement period. Grief is a complex process involving emotional, psychological, and social adjustments after a loss. Evidence-based hospice practice emphasizes ongoing bereavement support, especially during the weeks and months following death when family members may experience intense grief reactions, depression, or isolation. Follow-up contact allows the nurse to assess coping mechanisms, risk for complicated grief, and need for additional support services. Therefore, structured follow-up is considered a priority intervention in hospice nursing care.

(Correct Option 2). Checking on the family 1 to 2 months after the death is a priority because grief often intensifies after the initial period of support ends. This intervention provides an opportunity to assess long-term coping, emotional well-being, and need for counseling or community resources. It aligns with hospice standards of providing continued bereavement care and helps prevent complications such as prolonged or unresolved grief.

(Option 1). Attending the funeral or visitation may be supportive but is not a priority nursing intervention. It is a personal choice and does not provide structured or ongoing assessment of the family’s grieving process.

(Option 3). Ensuring arrangements reflect the client’s wishes is important before or immediately after death, but it is not a follow-up care priority. It does not address the family’s ongoing emotional needs during bereavement.

(Option 4). Encouraging the family to dispose of belongings quickly is inappropriate and may interfere with the natural grieving process. Families should be allowed to process grief at their own pace without pressure.

Educational Objective
Hospice nursing prioritizes ongoing bereavement support for families after a client’s death. Follow-up contact helps assess coping, emotional health, and risk for complicated grief, ensuring appropriate support and interventions are provided.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Hospice and Palliative Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Attend the client’s funeral service or visitation.
Check on the family 1 to 2 months after the death of the client.
Make sure the arrangements are what the client wanted.
Help the family member dispose of the client’s belongings as soon as possible.

questionList.questionText: The nurse is attempting to start an intravenous (IV) line in an elderly client who is dehydrated. After two unsuccessful attempts, which intervention should the nurse implement?
questionList.explanationText:
Intravenous (IV) access in elderly and dehydrated clients can be challenging due to fragile veins, decreased skin turgor, and reduced vascular volume. Repeated unsuccessful attempts can lead to vein damage, pain, increased risk of infection, and patient distress. Nursing standards and safety guidelines recommend limiting IV insertion attempts (usually no more than two) before seeking assistance. This promotes patient safety, comfort, and preservation of venous access. Utilizing another skilled nurse can increase the likelihood of successful insertion while minimizing harm.

(Correct Option 3). Asking a second nurse to attempt to start the IV is the safest and most appropriate action after two failed attempts. This follows clinical guidelines to prevent excessive trauma to veins and ensures that a more experienced or differently skilled nurse may successfully obtain access. It prioritizes patient safety, comfort, and effective treatment for dehydration.

(Option 1). Continuing to attempt IV insertion after multiple failures can cause vein damage, bruising, and increased patient discomfort. It does not follow best practice guidelines and may delay appropriate care.

(Option 2). Oral fluid replacement may not be appropriate for a dehydrated elderly client who requires rapid fluid resuscitation. IV access is often necessary for effective and timely treatment.

(Option 4). Applying cold packs can cause vasoconstriction, making veins harder to access. Warm compresses, not cold, are typically used to promote vasodilation and improve IV insertion success.

Educational Objective
After two unsuccessful IV attempts, nurses should seek assistance from another qualified provider to reduce patient harm and improve success rates. This aligns with patient safety and evidence-based practice guidelines.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Intravenous Therapy

answerChoiceList.choice: Keep trying to get a patent IV access.
Ask the HCP to order oral fluid replacement.
Ask a second nurse to attempt to start the IV.
Place cold packs on the client’s arms for comfort.

questionList.questionText: The client diagnosed with chronic kidney disease (CKD), and who has a left forearm graft, is assigned to the nurse and unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP). Which action by the UAP requires immediate intervention by the nurse?
questionList.explanationText:
Chronic kidney disease (CKD) often requires hemodialysis access through an arteriovenous (AV) graft or fistula, which is typically placed in the forearm. This access is vital for dialysis and must be protected to maintain patency and prevent complications. Applying pressure, such as taking blood pressure or drawing blood from the access arm, can lead to thrombosis, decreased blood flow, and potential loss of the access site. Nurses must ensure that all healthcare personnel avoid any procedures that compromise the graft. Immediate intervention is required if improper actions threaten the integrity of the vascular access.

(Correct Option 2). Taking blood pressure on the client’s left arm with an AV graft is unsafe and can cause compression of the graft, reduced blood flow, and possible clot formation. This action directly endangers the dialysis access, making it a priority for immediate intervention to prevent serious complications.

(Option 1). Avoiding soap while bathing is not a critical safety issue. In fact, gentle cleansing is appropriate, but this action does not pose immediate harm compared to compromising the AV graft.

(Option 3). Advising the client to avoid chips relates to dietary sodium restriction in CKD. While UAPs should not provide dietary teaching, this does not present an immediate threat to patient safety.

(Option 4). Measuring a scant amount of urine is an appropriate observation and reporting task for a UAP. It provides useful information about renal function and does not require intervention.

Educational Objective
In clients with AV grafts or fistulas, the access arm must be protected. Avoid blood pressure measurements, venipunctures, or constriction on that arm to prevent thrombosis and loss of dialysis access.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Chronic Kidney Disease

answerChoiceList.choice: The UAP avoids using soap while bathing the client.
The UAP takes the BP on the client’s left arm.
The UAP tells the client she should not eat chips.
The UAP measures a scant amount of urine in the BSC.

questionList.questionText: The nurse is observing the unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP) provide care to a client with an indwelling catheter. Which action by the UAP warrants immediate intervention by the nurse?
questionList.explanationText:
Indwelling urinary catheters require meticulous care to prevent catheter-associated urinary tract infections (CAUTIs) and mechanical complications. Proper catheter management includes securing the tubing, maintaining a closed drainage system, ensuring unobstructed urine flow, and performing hygiene with appropriate infection control measures. Failure to secure the catheter tubing can lead to tension on the catheter, urethral trauma, accidental dislodgement, and increased risk of infection. Nursing supervision of UAPs is essential to ensure adherence to these safety protocols and to prevent harm to the client.

(Correct Option 1). Not securing the catheter tubing to the client’s leg is unsafe because it allows movement and traction on the catheter. This can cause urethral injury, discomfort, and potential dislodgement. It also increases the risk of infection due to catheter movement. Immediate intervention is required to ensure the catheter is properly stabilized.

(Option 2). Wearing gloves during catheter care is correct practice and helps maintain infection control and standard precautions. This action does not require intervention.

(Option 3). Positioning the collection bag on the side of the bed is acceptable as long as it remains below the level of the bladder to prevent backflow. This action alone does not indicate unsafe care.

(Option 4). Washing hands before providing catheter care is appropriate and aligns with infection prevention guidelines. This is a correct action and should be encouraged.

Educational Objective
Proper catheter care requires securement of tubing, maintenance of a closed system, and adherence to infection control practices. Failure to secure the catheter can lead to trauma, dislodgement, and infection, requiring immediate nursing intervention.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Urinary Catheter Care

answerChoiceList.choice: The UAP does not secure the tubing to the client’s leg with tape.
The UAP wears gloves when providing catheter care to the client.
The UAP positions the collection bag on the side of the client’s bed.
The UAP cares for the client’s catheter after washing his or her hands.

questionList.questionText: The elderly patient diagnosed with heart failure is scheduled to receive a unit of packed red blood cells (PRBCs) in 3.5 hours. The PRBCs are prepared in 350 mL of solution. At what rate should the nurse set the pump in mL/min?
questionList.explanationText:
Intravenous infusion rate calculations are essential in ensuring safe administration of fluids and blood products, especially in clients with heart failure, where fluid overload can lead to pulmonary edema and worsening cardiac function. The nurse must calculate the rate accurately using the formula: Flow rate (mL/min) = Total volume (mL) ÷ Total time (minutes). First, convert hours into minutes (3.5 hours = 210 minutes). Then divide the total volume (350 mL) by time (210 minutes) to determine the correct infusion rate. Accurate calculations help prevent complications such as circulatory overload or under-infusion.

(Correct Option 3). The correct calculation is 350 mL ÷ 210 minutes = 1.7 mL/min. This ensures the PRBCs are infused safely over the prescribed time, reducing the risk of fluid overload in a patient with heart failure.

(Option 1). 1.2 mL/min is incorrect and would result in a slower infusion rate, delaying the administration of PRBCs and potentially affecting treatment outcomes.

(Option 2). 2.5 mL/min is too fast and could lead to fluid overload, especially dangerous in patients with compromised cardiac function.

(Option 4). 3.0 mL/min is significantly higher than the correct rate and increases the risk of circulatory overload, hypertension, and pulmonary edema.

Educational Objective
To calculate IV infusion rates, divide the total volume by total time in minutes. Accurate calculations are critical in preventing fluid overload or inadequate therapy, particularly in patients with conditions like heart failure.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: IV Flow Rate Calculation

answerChoiceList.choice: 1.2 mL/min
2.5 mL/min
1.7 mL/min
3.0 mL/min

questionList.questionText: Which interventions should the nurse delegate to the unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP) when caring for the client who is 2 days postoperative open surgery of the kidney? Select all that apply.
questionList.explanationText:
Delegation in nursing involves assigning tasks to appropriate personnel while maintaining accountability and patient safety. According to delegation principles, tasks given to UAPs must be routine, non-invasive, and not require clinical judgment or teaching. Nurses are responsible for assessment, evaluation, and patient education, while UAPs can perform tasks such as basic care, vital signs, hygiene, ambulation, and output measurement. Postoperative clients require close monitoring, so tasks involving assessment of complications or teaching must remain with the nurse, while stable routine care can be safely delegated.

(Correct Options 3, 4, 5). Taking and recording vital signs, emptying the catheter bag, and assisting with ambulation are appropriate tasks for UAPs. These are routine, standardized activities that do not require nursing judgment. Monitoring vital signs provides essential data, emptying the catheter ensures proper output tracking, and ambulation promotes recovery and prevention of complications such as deep vein thrombosis.

(Option 1). Explaining the PCA pump requires patient education and clinical knowledge, which must be performed by the nurse. Teaching is outside the scope of UAP responsibilities.

(Option 2). Assessing the surgical dressing for drainage involves clinical assessment and interpretation of findings, which is a nursing responsibility and cannot be delegated to a UAP.

Educational Objective
Nurses should delegate routine, non-assessment, and non-teaching tasks to UAPs, such as vital signs, ambulation, and output measurement. Tasks involving assessment, evaluation, or education must remain with the nurse to ensure patient safety.

questionList.questionText: The client with open surgery of the kidney has an AP 118 and B/P 88/58. Which intervention should the nurse implement first?
questionList.explanationText:
Postoperative complications following kidney surgery can include hemorrhage, hypovolemia, and shock. The client’s vital signs—tachycardia (AP 118) and hypotension (B/P 88/58)—are classic signs of hypovolemic shock, likely due to internal or external bleeding. In such cases, rapid recognition and immediate intervention are critical to prevent organ failure and death. According to nursing priority frameworks such as ABCs (Airway, Breathing, Circulation) and Maslow’s hierarchy, circulation issues take precedence. The nurse must act promptly by notifying the healthcare provider to initiate emergency interventions such as fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, or surgical intervention.

(Correct Option 3). Notifying the client’s surgeon immediately is the priority because the client is showing signs of hemodynamic instability and possible hemorrhage. This situation requires urgent medical intervention, and delaying notification could lead to rapid deterioration and life-threatening complications.

(Option 1). Obtaining a pulse oximeter reading provides additional data but does not address the immediate life-threatening issue of circulatory compromise. It is not the priority action.

(Option 2). Checking hemoglobin and hematocrit levels can help confirm bleeding but takes time and does not provide immediate intervention. The client requires urgent action before laboratory confirmation.

(Option 4). Monitoring urine output is important for assessing kidney function and perfusion, but it is not the first priority in a client with signs of shock and instability.

Educational Objective
In postoperative clients showing signs of hypotension and tachycardia, suspect hypovolemic shock. The priority is to notify the healthcare provider immediately to initiate life-saving interventions and stabilize the client.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Postoperative Complications

answerChoiceList.choice: Obtain the client’s pulse oximeter reading.
Check the client’s last hemoglobin and hematocrit.
Notify the client’s surgeon immediately.
Monitor the client’s urine output.

questionList.questionText:
The 88-year-old female client is complaining of urinary frequency and dribbling. Which nursing interventions should be implemented? Rank in order of performance.

a. Have the unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP) make “potty” rounds on the client every 2 hours.
b. Give the client perineal pads to place inside her underwear.
c. Place an absorbent pad on the client’s bed.
d. Put a bedside commode at the client’s bedside.
e. Instruct the client in providing a clean-catch urine specimen.
questionList.explanationText:
Urinary frequency and dribbling in an older adult may reflect urge incontinence, overflow incontinence, impaired mobility, or an underlying urinary tract infection. Nursing care should first focus on promoting safe and immediate access to toileting, then obtaining needed assessment data to identify the cause, and finally implementing measures that protect skin integrity and preserve dignity. Interventions that reduce episodes of incontinence, support elimination, and prevent complications such as moisture-associated skin damage are prioritized before routine convenience measures. The nurse should therefore begin with the least restrictive, most client-centered action that improves voiding access, followed by diagnostic evaluation and supportive continence care.

(Correct Option 3). The correct order is D, E, B, C, A. Placing a bedside commode first gives the client immediate access to toileting and may reduce incontinence episodes caused by urgency or limited mobility. Next, obtaining a clean-catch urine specimen helps evaluate for infection or other urinary pathology contributing to the symptoms. After assessment data are obtained, providing perineal pads supports dignity and manages leakage, and placing an absorbent pad on the bed protects linens and skin. Finally, scheduling UAP potty rounds every 2 hours is an ongoing supportive intervention that helps with continence training and prevention of future episodes.

(Option 1). This sequence is incorrect because it places containment measures such as pads and bed protection before improving the client’s immediate access to toileting and before obtaining needed assessment data. Nursing care should first address the client’s elimination needs and investigate the cause rather than starting with passive leakage management.

(Option 2). This order is incorrect because it delays the bedside commode and does not prioritize the initial intervention that can most directly reduce urinary accidents. It also gives supportive and protective measures too early, before the nurse gathers a urine specimen to assess for contributing pathology such as a urinary tract infection.

(Option 4). This option is incorrect because it starts with delegated potty rounds before the nurse first establishes immediate toileting access and obtains diagnostic information. Delegated scheduled toileting is useful, but it is not the first action when the nurse still needs to facilitate voiding and assess the reason for the new urinary symptoms.

Educational Objective
When caring for an older adult with urinary frequency and dribbling, prioritize interventions that improve toileting access and obtain needed assessment data before long-term supportive measures. Protecting skin integrity, maintaining dignity, and using scheduled toileting are important follow-up strategies in continence care.

answerChoiceList.choice: B, C, D, E, A
E, D, B, A, C
D, E, B, C, A

questionList.questionText: The nurse in the dialysis center is initiating the morning dialysis run. Which client should the nurse assess first?
questionList.explanationText:
Prioritization in nursing care is guided by frameworks such as ABCs (Airway, Breathing, Circulation), Maslow’s hierarchy, and identifying life-threatening complications. In dialysis patients, complications such as peritonitis, fluid overload, vascular access failure, and electrolyte imbalances must be quickly recognized. A client on peritoneal dialysis who has a hard, rigid abdomen may be experiencing peritonitis, a serious infection that can rapidly progress to sepsis. This condition requires immediate assessment and intervention. Therefore, the nurse must prioritize clients exhibiting acute, life-threatening signs over those with chronic or less urgent issues.

(Correct Option 4). A client on peritoneal dialysis with a hard, rigid abdomen is showing signs of peritonitis, which is a medical emergency. Immediate assessment is required to prevent infection progression, sepsis, and potential organ failure. This makes it the highest priority.

(Option 1). Low hemoglobin and hematocrit levels indicate anemia, which is common in chronic kidney disease. Although important, this is a chronic condition and not immediately life-threatening.

(Option 2). Absence of a thrill or bruit suggests vascular access occlusion, which is serious but not immediately life-threatening compared to peritonitis. It requires prompt attention but not first priority.

(Option 3). A weight gain of 3.6 kg indicates fluid overload, and refusal of dialysis is concerning. However, this situation is less acute than signs of infection and can be addressed after stabilizing more critical clients.

Educational Objective
When prioritizing care, always assess clients with acute, life-threatening conditions first. In dialysis patients, signs of peritonitis (rigid abdomen) require immediate intervention due to risk of sepsis and rapid deterioration.

questionList.title: Dialysis Complications

The client who does not have a palpable thrill or auscultated bruit.
The client who is reporting a 3.6 kg weight gain and is refusing dialysis.
The client on peritoneal dialysis who is complaining of a hard, rigid abdomen.

questionList.questionText: The male client with chronic kidney disease has received the initial dose of erythropoietin, a biological response modifier, 1 week ago. Which data warrants the nurse to notify the healthcare provider?
questionList.explanationText:
Erythropoietin is a biological response modifier used in chronic kidney disease (CKD) to stimulate red blood cell production and treat anemia. While effective, it can cause significant side effects, most notably hypertension, due to increased blood viscosity and vasoconstriction. Monitoring for adverse effects such as elevated blood pressure, thromboembolic events, and rapid rise in hemoglobin is critical. Severe hypertension can lead to stroke, myocardial infarction, or heart failure, making it a priority finding that requires immediate notification of the healthcare provider.

(Correct Option 3). A blood pressure of 184/102 indicates severe hypertension, which is a known and dangerous adverse effect of erythropoietin therapy. This requires immediate intervention to prevent life-threatening complications such as stroke or cardiac events.

(Option 1). A pulse oximeter reading of 95% is within acceptable limits and does not indicate a need for intervention or provider notification.

(Option 2). A platelet count of 155,000 is within the normal range and does not suggest any complication related to erythropoietin therapy.

(Option 4). A temperature of 99.8°F is slightly elevated but not considered a fever and is not an urgent concern in this context.

Educational Objective
Erythropoietin therapy can cause severe hypertension. Nurses must monitor blood pressure closely and notify the healthcare provider immediately if significant elevations occur to prevent serious complications.

questionList.questionText: The nurse is developing a nursing care plan for the client diagnosed with chronic kidney disease. Which nursing problem should be addressed first?
questionList.explanationText:
Chronic kidney disease (CKD) leads to progressive loss of kidney function, resulting in fluid retention, electrolyte imbalances, and accumulation of waste products. One of the most critical complications is excess fluid volume, which can cause hypertension, pulmonary edema, and heart failure. According to prioritization frameworks such as ABCs (Airway, Breathing, Circulation) and Maslow’s hierarchy of needs, physiological problems affecting circulation and oxygenation take priority over psychosocial or educational concerns. Fluid overload directly impacts cardiovascular and respiratory systems, making it the most urgent nursing problem to address first.

(Correct Option 4). Excess fluid volume is the priority because it can lead to life-threatening complications such as pulmonary edema and decreased cardiac output. Managing fluid balance helps stabilize the client’s circulatory and respiratory status, which are essential for survival.

(Option 1). Self-care deficit is important for long-term management but does not pose an immediate threat to the client’s physiological stability or survival.

(Option 2). Knowledge deficit relates to patient education, which is essential but should be addressed after stabilizing urgent physiological issues.

(Option 3). Chronic pain affects comfort and quality of life but is not immediately life-threatening compared to fluid overload in CKD.

Educational Objective
In CKD, prioritize problems that affect circulation and oxygenation. Excess fluid volume is a critical issue that must be addressed first to prevent cardiopulmonary complications and ensure patient stability.

questionList.title: Chronic Kidney Disease

answerChoiceList.choice: Self-care deficit.

questionList.questionText: The client with chronic kidney disease is placed on a fluid restriction of 1,500 milliliters per day. On the 7 a.m. to 7 p.m. shift the client drank an 8-ounce cup of coffee, 8 ounces of juice, 16 ounces of tea, and 8 ounces of water with medications. What amount of fluid can the 7 p.m. to 7 a.m. nurse give to the client?
questionList.explanationText:
Fluid management in chronic kidney disease (CKD) is essential because impaired kidney function leads to fluid retention, electrolyte imbalance, and risk of fluid overload. Excess fluid can result in hypertension, pulmonary edema, and heart failure. Nurses must accurately calculate intake using the conversion 1 ounce = 30 mL. The client consumed 8 oz (240 mL) coffee, 8 oz (240 mL) juice, 16 oz (480 mL) tea, and 8 oz (240 mL) water, totaling 1,200 mL. With a daily allowance of 1,500 mL, the remaining fluid allowed is 300 mL. Accurate calculations are critical to prevent life-threatening complications.

(Correct Option 2). The remaining fluid allowance is 300 mL because the client has already consumed 1,200 mL during the day shift. Subtracting from the total prescribed 1,500 mL leaves 300 mL for the night shift, ensuring safe fluid balance.

(Option 1). 200 mL is incorrect because it underestimates the remaining allowance and may unnecessarily restrict the client, potentially leading to dehydration.

(Option 3). 500 mL is incorrect because it overestimates the remaining allowance and could result in fluid overload, increasing the risk of pulmonary edema.

(Option 4). 700 mL is significantly incorrect and would exceed the prescribed fluid restriction, placing the client at high risk for cardiovascular and respiratory complications.

Educational Objective
In CKD, strict fluid management is crucial. Nurses must accurately convert ounces to milliliters and calculate remaining intake to prevent fluid overload and associated complications.

questionList.title: Fluid Balance Calculation

answerChoiceList.choice: 200 mL
300 mL
500 mL

questionList.questionText: The client receiving dialysis is complaining of being dizzy and light-headed. Which priority intervention should the nurse implement?
questionList.explanationText:
During hemodialysis, fluid is removed from the bloodstream through a process called ultrafiltration. Excessive or rapid removal of fluid can lead to intravascular volume depletion, causing hypotension. Symptoms such as dizziness, light-headedness, nausea, and weakness are common indicators of this complication. The priority nursing intervention is to address the cause of hypotension immediately by adjusting the dialysis settings. Early recognition and prompt intervention are essential to prevent further complications such as shock or decreased organ perfusion.

(Correct Option 2). Decreasing the volume of blood or fluid being removed directly addresses the underlying cause of hypotension during dialysis. By reducing ultrafiltration, the nurse helps restore hemodynamic stability and prevents worsening symptoms. This is the most immediate and appropriate priority intervention.

(Option 1). Placing the client in reverse Trendelenburg position would actually worsen hypotension by reducing venous return. Instead, a position that promotes blood flow to vital organs would be more appropriate, making this option incorrect.

(Option 3). Administering a bolus of 0.9% saline may be appropriate if hypotension persists, but it is not the first-line intervention. Adjusting the dialysis settings should be done first before adding fluid.

(Option 4). Notifying the healthcare provider is important but is not the immediate priority. The nurse must first implement independent nursing interventions to stabilize the patient before escalation.

Educational Objective
Clients undergoing dialysis are at risk for hypotension due to rapid fluid removal. The priority intervention is to reduce ultrafiltration to stabilize blood pressure and prevent complications. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

questionList.questionText: The client is NPO and is receiving total parenteral nutrition (TPN) via a subclavian line. Which precautions should the nurse implement? Select all that apply.
questionList.explanationText:
Total parenteral nutrition (TPN) is a highly concentrated, hypertonic solution containing glucose, amino acids, vitamins, and electrolytes administered through a central line. Due to its high glucose concentration, clients are at risk for hyperglycemia, infection, and fluid imbalance. Strict monitoring is essential, including daily weights, intake and output tracking, and aseptic technique during tubing changes. TPN must be administered using an infusion pump rather than gravity to ensure accurate delivery, and blood glucose should be monitored more frequently than every 24 hours due to the risk of rapid fluctuations.

(Correct Option 3). Daily weight measurement is essential because it provides the most accurate indicator of fluid balance and nutritional status. Monitoring trends helps detect complications such as fluid overload or dehydration early.

(Correct Option 4). Changing IV tubing with every TPN bag is necessary to maintain strict aseptic technique and reduce the risk of catheter-related bloodstream infections, which are a major complication of TPN therapy.

(Correct Option 5). Monitoring intake and output every shift helps assess fluid balance, kidney function, and effectiveness of therapy. It is critical for detecting imbalances early in clients receiving TPN.

(Option 1). TPN should never be administered via a gravity line because it requires precise control. An infusion pump is mandatory to prevent rapid infusion, which can cause serious complications such as hyperglycemia or fluid overload.

(Option 2). Monitoring blood glucose every 24 hours is insufficient. Clients on TPN require frequent glucose monitoring (often every 4–6 hours) due to the high glucose content, making this option incorrect.

Educational Objective
Clients receiving TPN require strict monitoring for infection, fluid balance, and glucose control. Key nursing precautions include daily weights, intake and output monitoring, and changing tubing with each bag to prevent complications. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

questionList.title: Parenteral Nutrition

answerChoiceList.choice: Place the client’s TPN on a gravity intravenous line.
Monitor the client’s blood glucose every 24 hours.
Weigh the client daily, first thing in the morning.
Change the client’s IV tubing with every TPN bag administered.

questionList.questionText:
The client has received IV solutions for 3 days through a 20-gauge IV catheter placed in the left cephalic vein. On morning rounds the nurse notes the IV site is tender to palpation, it is edematous, and a red streak has formed. Which interventions should the nurse implement? Rank in priority order.

a. Start a new IV in the right hand.
b. Discontinue the intravenous line.
c. Complete an incident record.
d. Place a warm washcloth over the site.
e. Document the situation in the client’s chart.
questionList.explanationText:
Phlebitis is an inflammation of the vein often caused by prolonged IV therapy, mechanical irritation from the catheter, or chemical irritation from IV fluids. The signs include pain, edema, erythema, warmth, and red streaking along the vein. The priority nursing action is to stop the source of irritation by discontinuing the IV line immediately. After removal, symptomatic treatment such as warm compresses helps reduce inflammation and promote circulation. Proper documentation ensures continuity of care, while incident reports are completed last as they are not part of the medical record but used for quality improvement.

(Correct Option 3). The correct sequence is: Discontinue IV → Start new IV → Apply warm compress → Document → Complete incident report. The nurse must first remove the IV catheter to prevent further vein damage. Establishing a new IV ensures continued therapy. Applying warmth helps relieve inflammation and discomfort. Documentation follows for legal and communication purposes, and finally, the incident report is completed for institutional tracking.

(Option 1). Starting a new IV first is incorrect because the priority is to remove the source of injury. Initiating another IV without addressing the current complication may worsen the condition and delay proper treatment.

(Option 2). While discontinuing the IV is correct, placing it second in sequence is incorrect because it must be the first priority action when signs of phlebitis are present.

(Option 4). Applying a warm compress is appropriate but should only be done after discontinuing the IV. It is not the initial priority intervention.

(Option 5). Documentation is essential but comes after patient care interventions. Immediate clinical actions always take precedence over recording.

Educational Objective
In cases of IV complications like phlebitis, always prioritize removing the source of harm first. Follow with supportive care, ensure continuation of therapy if needed, and complete documentation and reporting last.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: IV Therapy Complications

questionList.questionText: The nurse and unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP) are caring for a group of clients. Which nursing intervention should the nurse perform?
questionList.explanationText:
Delegation in nursing requires the nurse to assign tasks appropriately based on the scope of practice and level of training. Unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP) can perform routine, non-invasive, and standardized tasks such as measuring output or documenting intake and output. However, tasks that involve assessment, teaching, clinical judgment, or evaluation must be performed by the registered nurse. Client education, especially regarding fluid restrictions and disease management, requires critical thinking and individualized planning, making it a nursing responsibility.

(Correct Option 3). Instructing the client on appropriate fluid restrictions involves teaching and evaluation of understanding, which are key components of the nursing process. This requires clinical judgment and patient-specific education, and therefore must be performed by the nurse.

(Option 1). Measuring output from an indwelling catheter is a routine, non-invasive task that can be safely delegated to UAP. It does not require clinical judgment or interpretation.

(Option 2). Recording intake and output is also a standardized task that can be delegated to UAP, as it involves documentation without analysis or decision-making.

(Option 4). Providing water to a client may be delegated depending on the situation, but it requires the nurse to first assess the client’s condition and restrictions. However, it does not involve the same level of teaching and clinical judgment as option C, making it less appropriate as the nurse-only task.

Educational Objective
The nurse must retain tasks involving assessment, teaching, and clinical judgment, while routine and non-invasive tasks can be delegated to UAP. Patient education is always a nursing responsibility. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

questionList.title: Delegation and Prioritization

Record the client’s intake and output on the I&O sheet.
Instruct the client on appropriate fluid restrictions.

questionList.questionText: The nurse emptied 2,340 mL from the drainage bag of a continuous irrigation of a client who had a transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP). The amount of irrigation in the hanging bag was 3,000 mL at the beginning of the shift. There was 1,550 mL left in the bag 8 hours later. What is the correct urine output at the end of the 8 hours?
questionList.explanationText:
After a transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP), clients often receive continuous bladder irrigation (CBI) to prevent clot formation and maintain catheter patency. To calculate the true urine output, the nurse must subtract the amount of irrigation fluid infused from the total drainage output. This requires understanding that the drainage bag contains a mixture of urine and irrigation solution. Accurate calculation is critical to assess renal function and fluid balance. In this case, the amount of irrigation infused is calculated by subtracting the remaining fluid from the initial amount, and then subtracting that value from the total drainage.

(Correct Option 3). The correct urine output is calculated as follows: irrigation infused = 3000 mL − 1550 mL = 1450 mL. Then subtract irrigation from total drainage: 2340 mL − 1450 mL = 890 mL. This represents the actual urine output, excluding irrigation fluid.

(Option 1). This option is incorrect because it does not properly subtract the irrigation fluid from the total output, leading to an inaccurate estimation of urine production.

(Option 2). This value may result from partial calculation or misinterpretation of the irrigation volume, but it fails to correctly apply the formula needed to determine true urine output.

(Option 4). This option overestimates urine output by not accounting for the full amount of irrigation infused, which can lead to incorrect clinical assessment of fluid balance.

Educational Objective
To calculate urine output during continuous bladder irrigation, subtract the irrigation infused from the total drainage. This ensures accurate assessment of kidney function and fluid status. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

questionList.title: Postoperative Care (TURP)

questionList.questionText: Which nursing diagnosis is priority for the client who has undergone a transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP)?
questionList.explanationText:
Transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) is a surgical procedure that involves removal of prostate tissue, which places the client at risk for several postoperative complications. The most immediate and life-threatening complication is hemorrhage, due to the highly vascular nature of the prostate. Excessive bleeding can lead to hypovolemia, shock, and decreased tissue perfusion. According to priority-setting frameworks such as Maslow’s hierarchy and ABCs (Airway, Breathing, Circulation), maintaining circulation and preventing blood loss takes precedence over other concerns such as infection or elimination issues.

(Correct Option 4). Potential for hemorrhage is the highest priority because it poses an immediate threat to life. Early signs such as bright red bleeding, increased drainage, and decreasing blood pressure require prompt intervention to prevent shock.

(Option 1). Potential for sexual dysfunction is a valid concern after TURP but is not a priority in the immediate postoperative period. It does not pose an immediate threat to the client’s survival.

(Option 2). Altered urinary elimination is expected after TURP due to catheterization and swelling. While important, it is not as urgent as preventing life-threatening complications like hemorrhage.

(Option 3). Potential for infection is always a concern with surgical procedures, but it typically develops over time and is not as immediately life-threatening as hemorrhage.

Educational Objective
After TURP, the priority nursing diagnosis is risk for hemorrhage due to the highly vascular surgical site. Preventing and detecting bleeding early is critical to avoid life-threatening complications. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

questionList.title: Postoperative Care (TURP)

questionList.questionText: The client is 1 day postoperative transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP). Which action by the unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP) warrants intervention by the nurse?
questionList.explanationText:
Delegation principles require that tasks involving assessment, evaluation, or clinical judgment must be performed by the nurse, while UAPs are limited to routine, non-invasive, and standardized tasks. After a TURP, clients often receive continuous bladder irrigation (CBI), which requires careful monitoring and adjustment based on urine color, presence of clots, and risk of hemorrhage. Adjusting irrigation flow is a critical nursing responsibility because improper regulation can lead to bladder distention, clot retention, or increased bleeding.

(Correct Option 1). Increasing the irrigation fluid to clear clots involves clinical judgment and assessment of bleeding. This action should only be performed by the nurse, making it inappropriate for UAP and requiring immediate intervention.

(Option 2). Elevating the scrotum on a towel roll is a comfort measure that helps reduce swelling and is appropriate for delegation to UAP. It does not require clinical judgment.

(Option 3). Emptying an indwelling urinary catheter bag is a routine task that can be safely delegated to UAP, as it does not involve assessment or decision-making.

(Option 4). Bringing ice water to the bedside is also a non-invasive supportive task that can be delegated, provided there are no fluid restrictions.

Educational Objective
UAPs should not perform tasks requiring assessment or clinical judgment. Adjusting bladder irrigation after TURP is a nursing responsibility due to the risk of bleeding and complications. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

questionList.questionText: The male client diagnosed with renal calculi is admitted to the medical unit. Which intervention should the nurse implement first?
questionList.explanationText:
Renal calculi (kidney stones) can cause severe obstruction of urine flow, leading to intense renal colic pain. The pain occurs due to ureteral spasm and increased pressure proximal to the obstruction. According to the nursing process and priority frameworks such as ABCs and Maslow’s hierarchy, the first step is always assessment before implementing interventions. Pain assessment is critical to determine the severity, location, and characteristics of the discomfort, which guides further treatment such as analgesia and hydration. Immediate assessment helps prevent complications and ensures appropriate care planning.

(Correct Option 2). Assessing the client’s pain is the priority because it is the first step of the nursing process (assessment). Understanding the intensity and nature of pain allows the nurse to implement appropriate interventions and evaluate effectiveness.

(Option 1). Asking the client to urinate in a urinal is important for monitoring output and possibly collecting stones, but it is not the immediate priority compared to assessing the client’s condition.

(Option 3). Increasing oral fluid intake helps facilitate stone passage, but it should only be done after assessing the client and ensuring there are no contraindications such as severe obstruction.

(Option 4). Straining the urine is necessary to catch stones for analysis, but it is not the first intervention. Assessment takes priority before implementing ongoing management strategies.

Educational Objective
In clients with renal calculi, pain assessment is the priority nursing action. Assessment guides appropriate interventions and ensures effective pain management and care planning. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

questionList.questionText: The female client with renal calculi is scheduled for a STAT kidney, ureter, bladder (KUB). Which statement by the client warrants intervention by the nurse?
questionList.explanationText:
A kidney, ureter, bladder (KUB) radiograph is a diagnostic imaging test used to visualize the urinary tract. It typically involves plain X-ray imaging and may expose the client to radiation. One of the most critical considerations before performing radiologic procedures is the possibility of pregnancy, as radiation exposure can cause teratogenic effects, fetal injury, or developmental abnormalities. Therefore, assessing for pregnancy status is a priority to ensure patient safety and prevent harm to a fetus.

(Correct Option 3). The statement "I have not had my period for 3 months" suggests a possible pregnancy, which requires immediate intervention. The nurse must notify the healthcare provider and delay the procedure until pregnancy is ruled out to avoid radiation exposure to the fetus.

(Option 1). Allergy to shellfish and iodine is relevant for procedures involving contrast dye, but a standard KUB does not typically use contrast, making this less concerning in this scenario.

(Option 2). Eating prior to a KUB is generally not contraindicated, as this imaging test does not usually require fasting. Therefore, this statement does not require immediate intervention.

(Option 4). Lower back pain is a common symptom of renal calculi and does not interfere with performing a KUB. It does not warrant intervention before the procedure.

Educational Objective
Before radiologic procedures, always assess for possible pregnancy. Radiation exposure can harm a developing fetus, making pregnancy screening a priority nursing responsibility. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

questionList.questionText: The client diagnosed with renal calculi is scheduled for a 24-hour urine specimen collection. Which interventions should the nurse implement? Select all that apply.
questionList.explanationText:
A 24-hour urine collection is used to evaluate kidney function and measure substances such as creatinine, protein, and electrolytes. Accurate collection is essential for valid results. The procedure requires discarding the first voided urine, then collecting all subsequent urine for 24 hours, including the final void at the end of the period. Proper handling involves placing urine in a designated container, often kept cool, and ensuring that all urine output is saved. Missing any specimen can lead to inaccurate results and misinterpretation of kidney function.

(Correct Option 3). Placing the client’s urine in an appropriate specimen container ensures that all urine is collected and preserved correctly for accurate analysis. Proper storage is essential to maintain specimen integrity.

(Correct Option 5). Posting signs to alert staff ensures that all urine output is saved during the collection period. This prevents accidental disposal and maintains accuracy of the test.

(Option 1). Keeping the client NPO is not required for a 24-hour urine collection. The client should maintain normal diet and fluid intake unless otherwise ordered.

(Option 2). The first voided urine should be discarded, not included. Including it would invalidate the timing and accuracy of the 24-hour collection.

(Option 4). Inserting an indwelling catheter is unnecessary and increases the risk of infection. It is not routinely done for urine collection unless specifically indicated.

Educational Objective
For a 24-hour urine collection, discard the first void, collect all urine for 24 hours, use proper containers, and ensure all staff are aware to save every specimen for accurate results. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

questionList.title: Diagnostic Procedures

Place client’s urine in an appropriate specimen container for 24 hours.
Insert an indwelling catheter in client after having the client empty the bladder.
Post signs on the client’s door alerting staff to save all of the client’s urine output.

questionList.questionText: The client diagnosed with renal calculi is 1 hour post-procedure lithotripsy. Which task is most appropriate for the nurse to delegate to the unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP)?
questionList.explanationText:
Delegation principles require that tasks assigned to unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP) must be non-invasive, routine, and not require clinical judgment. After lithotripsy, the client may pass stone fragments in the urine, so monitoring and collecting these fragments is important. Tasks involving assessment, interpretation, or evaluation such as analyzing urine characteristics or interpreting vital signs trends must be performed by the nurse. UAPs can assist with basic specimen collection and routine care under proper instruction.

(Correct Option 3). Straining the client’s urine and placing sediment in a sterile container is a routine, non-invasive task that can be safely delegated to UAP. It does not require interpretation, only proper technique and instruction.

(Option 1). Checking the amount, color, and consistency of urine requires assessment and clinical judgment, especially post-procedure, and must be performed by the nurse.

(Option 2). Transcribing healthcare provider orders is a licensed nursing responsibility and cannot be delegated to UAP.

(Option 4). Taking post-procedural vital signs may involve evaluation and interpretation in a potentially unstable client, making it more appropriate for the nurse rather than UAP in the immediate postoperative period.

Educational Objective
UAPs can perform routine tasks such as specimen collection, but nurses must retain responsibilities involving assessment, interpretation, and clinical judgment, especially after procedures. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

questionList.title: Delegation and Prioritization

answerChoiceList.choice: Tell the UAP to check the amount, color, and consistency of the client’s urine output.
Request the UAP to transcribe the client’s healthcare provider’s orders.
Instruct the UAP to strain the client’s urine and place any sediment in a sterile container.

questionList.questionText: The client had surgery to remove a kidney stone. Which of the following laboratory assessment data warrants intervention by the nurse?
questionList.explanationText:
Postoperative clients who undergo kidney stone removal are at risk for complications related to electrolyte imbalance, renal function changes, and infection. One critical electrolyte to monitor is potassium, as even slight elevations can affect cardiac conduction and neuromuscular function. Normal potassium levels range from 3.5 to 5.0 mEq/L, and levels above this indicate hyperkalemia, which can lead to life-threatening dysrhythmias. Other lab values such as mild hematuria, normal creatinine, and normal white blood cell counts may be expected or within normal limits postoperatively.

(Correct Option 1). A serum potassium level of 5.2 mEq/L is slightly elevated and indicates hyperkalemia, which requires prompt intervention. Elevated potassium can disrupt cardiac electrical activity, potentially leading to arrhythmias, making this a priority concern.

(Option 2). Blood in the urine (hematuria) is expected after kidney stone surgery due to irritation of the urinary tract. It is a common finding and does not require immediate intervention unless excessive.

(Option 3). A creatinine level of 1.2 mg/100 mL is within the normal range, indicating adequate renal function. It does not suggest a complication.

(Option 4). A white blood cell count of 9,500 mm/dL is within the normal range and does not indicate infection or inflammatory complications requiring intervention.

Educational Objective
After kidney surgery, monitoring potassium levels is critical because hyperkalemia can lead to life-threatening cardiac complications. Slight elevations must be addressed promptly. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

questionList.questionText: Which intervention should the nurse implement first for the client diagnosed with urinary incontinence?
questionList.explanationText:
Urinary incontinence is the involuntary leakage of urine and can result from various underlying causes such as overflow, stress, urge, or functional incontinence. The priority in nursing care is to first assess the underlying cause before implementing treatment interventions. Bladder assessment, including palpation, helps identify issues like urinary retention or bladder distention, which may contribute to overflow incontinence. Accurate assessment ensures appropriate and safe management rather than prematurely initiating medications or behavioral interventions.

(Correct Option 1). Palpating the bladder after an incontinent episode allows the nurse to assess for urinary retention or incomplete bladder emptying. This is essential because overflow incontinence may occur due to retained urine, and identifying this helps guide further interventions such as catheterization or medication adjustments. Assessment always precedes implementation in the nursing process, making this the priority action.

(Option 2). Administering oxybutynin, an anticholinergic agent, is inappropriate as a first step because medication should only be given after determining the type and cause of incontinence. Using anticholinergics without proper assessment may worsen conditions like urinary retention.

(Option 3). Ensuring the client does not sit or lie in urine is important for maintaining skin integrity and preventing infection, but it does not address the underlying cause. It is a supportive measure rather than a priority initial intervention.

(Option 4). Instructing the client to void every 2 hours is a bladder training technique, which is useful in managing certain types of incontinence. However, it should only be implemented after proper assessment of the client’s condition.

Educational Objective
In clients with urinary incontinence, assessment is the priority before implementing treatment. Identifying the underlying cause, such as urinary retention, ensures that interventions are safe, effective, and tailored to the client’s specific condition.
